sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “Set Your House in Order”; the Shadow Retreats; Fifteen Years Added

refs.note: Isaiah’s parallel includes Hezekiah’s psalm of thanksgiving, not preserved in Kings. The two accounts complement each other.
God relented from the judgment he had announced against Nineveh. The pattern: prophetic announcement is not always prophetic inevitability.

notes.note: God’s reversal of the death sentence teaches that prayer genuinely changes outcomes. God is not a fixed machine; he is a person who responds to persons. The mystery of divine sovereignty and human petition meet in this text. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: debēlat tĕʾēnîm (“a lump of figs”) — medicinal use of fig poultices is documented in the Ras Shamra texts from Ugarit (14th–12th century BC) and in Egyptian medical papyri. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The fig poultice prescribed by Isaiah is attested in ANE medical texts from Ugarit and Mesopotamia. Figs were commonly used to treat boils and abscesses. The prescription combines prophetic authority with practical medicine — divine healing works through natural means.
The “stairway of Ahaz” may be a sundial or stepped structure used for timekeeping. The retreat of the shadow by ten steps reverses the normal course of the day — an observable astronomical anomaly.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Hezekiah’s tears are not weakness but genuine engagement with God. The king who faced Sennacherib with faith now faces death with grief. Both responses are authentic; both receive divine attention. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Hezekiah becomes terminally ill. Isaiah tells him: “Put your house in order, because you are going to die.” Hezekiah turns to the wall and weeps, praying: “Remember how I have walked before you faithfully.” Before Isaiah leaves the middle court, God reverses the sentence: fifteen more years, plus deliverance from Assyria. The sign: the shadow on Ahaz’s stairway retreats ten steps. The miracle confirms the word; the word reverses the death sentence. Prayer changes outcomes — even outcomes announced by a prophet.

heb.paragraph: Dāraš is the Chronicler's signature verb — appearing more than 40 times in 1-2 Chronicles, far more frequently than in Kings. 'To seek the LORD' is the defining act of faithful kingship in the Chronicler's theology. Kings who seek (dāraš) prosper; kings who forsake (ʿāzaḇ) are abandoned. This binary drives the entire narrative.

sections.header: Verses 12–21 — Merodach-Baladan’s Embassy; Hezekiah Shows Everything; Isaiah’s Warning

refs.note: The parallel in Isaiah is nearly identical. The placement at the end of “First Isaiah” (chs 1–39) transitions to the Babylonian exile focus of “Second Isaiah” (chs 40–55).
Nebuchadnezzar carries off “all the treasures of the temple and the royal palace” — Isaiah’s prophecy fulfilled exactly.

notes.note: The Babylonian prophecy reminds us that the consequences of pride often fall on subsequent generations. Hezekiah’s display of wealth planted the seed; his great-grandsons reaped the harvest. Parental sin has intergenerational consequences. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Babylonian name Merodach-Baladan (Marduk-apal-iddina) means “Marduk has given a son.” His documented rebellions against Assyria confirm the diplomatic context of his embassy to Hezekiah. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Merodach-Baladan (Marduk-apal-iddina II) was a Chaldean who seized the Babylonian throne twice (721–710 and 703 BC). His embassy to Hezekiah was diplomatic alliance-building against Assyria. Merodach-Baladan is well attested in both Assyrian and Babylonian records.

Hezekiah’s tunnel (Siloam Tunnel) is one of the most spectacular archaeological confirmations in biblical history. The 533-metre tunnel was carved through solid rock to channel water from the Gihon Spring inside the city walls. The Siloam Inscription, discovered in 1880, describes the tunnellers meeting in the middle.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Hezekiah’s final words in the narrative — “Will there not be peace and security in my lifetime?” — reveal the limits of even the best king. His faith is real but his moral vision is bounded by his own lifespan. The contrast with Abraham (who blessed nations he would never see) is implicit.

hist.context: Merodach-Baladan of Babylon sends envoys with letters and a gift, ostensibly congratulating Hezekiah on his recovery. Hezekiah shows them everything: the treasury, the armoury, the storehouses — “there was nothing in his palace or in all his kingdom that Hezekiah did not show them.” Isaiah asks: “What did they see?” Answer: everything. Isaiah’s prophecy: “Everything in your palace will be carried off to Babylon.” Hezekiah’s response is chilling: “The word of the LORD you have spoken is good” — because it won’t happen in his lifetime. The seed of Judah’s destruction is planted by its greatest king’s greatest weakness: pride.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the Lord,ESVThen Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD, saying,NIV\u201cRemember, Lord, how I have walked before you faithfully and with wholehearted devotion and have done what is good in your eyes.\u201d And Hezekiah wept bitterly.ESV\u201cNow, O LORD, please remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness and with a whole heart, and have done what is good in your sight.\u201d And Hezekiah wept bitterly.
